document.source_title: Free up fertiliser supplies to avert global food crisis, Yvette Cooper urges
document.author: Yvette Cooper
document.summary: Yvette Cooper, the UK's foreign secretary, has called for urgent action to free up global fertiliser supplies to prevent a looming food crisis exacerbated by the war in Iran. The conflict has disrupted fertiliser shipments through the strait of Hormuz, leading to rising food prices and significant impacts on farming in the UK, Europe, and the developing world. Cooper emphasized the need for international cooperation to address these challenges and ensure food security.
key_takeaways: Urgent need to free up fertiliser supplies to avoid a global food crisis.
The war in Iran is disrupting fertiliser shipments, affecting food prices.
Developing countries are facing the worst impacts of rising costs.
The UK has cut overseas aid, impacting global stability.
The World Food Programme warns of increased food insecurity.
New partnerships are needed to enhance international development efforts.
document.why_it_matters: The situation highlights the interconnectedness of global food security and international relations, particularly how conflicts can disrupt essential supplies. Cooper's call for action underscores the importance of international cooperation in addressing food insecurity and supporting vulnerable populations, especially in developing countries.

claims.claim_text: Global fertiliser supplies must be freed up within weeks to avoid disaster.
claims.evidence_text: Global fertiliser supplies must be freed up within weeks to avoid disaster, with harvests suffering and food prices rising, the UK’s foreign secretary, Yvette Cooper has said.

claims.claim_text: The war in Iran has frozen shipments of fertiliser through the strait of Hormuz.
claims.evidence_text: The war in Iran has frozen shipments of fertiliser through the strait of Hormuz, creating a supply crunch that has already damaged farming in the UK, Europe and the US.

claims.claim_text: The World Food Programme estimates that almost 45 million more people could fall into acute food insecurity.
claims.evidence_text: The World Food Programme estimates that almost 45 million more people could fall into acute food insecurity if the Iran conflict does not end by the middle of this year.

events.evidence_text: before a conference of governments in London to discuss overseas aid and development.

metrics.name: UK overseas aid percentage
metrics.evidence_text: The UK cut aid from 0.5% of gross national income under the last government – itself a cut from 0.7% under the previous Labour government – to 0.3%.

metrics.name: Climate finance cut
metrics.evidence_text: Climate finance, which helps developing countries protect their ecosystems and become more resilient to the impacts of extreme weather, has been cut to £2bn a year over the next three years.

metrics.name: Investment for climate in emerging markets
metrics.evidence_text: British International Investment will announce £4.6bn for climate investment in emerging markets.
